mainText:
Good morning. Here's your Breeze communications briefing for Thursday, April 16.

No new Breeze coverage in the last 24 hours.

Now for industry and competitor news.

Allegiant Air and Sun Country Airlines announced on April 15th that the U.S. Department of Transportation has approved their joint exemption application, clearing the regulatory path for both ultra-low-cost carriers to continue operating independently under common ownership when their 1.5 billion dollar merger closes — expected around May 13th following a shareholder vote on May 8th. Network planning should map where the combined Allegiant and Sun Country footprint overlaps with Breeze leisure routes and evaluate the competitive implications before the deal closes.

C.B.S. News reported that the House passed the A.L.E.R.T. Act on April 15th by a vote of 396 to 10, a bipartisan aviation safety bill requiring military aircraft near major airports to install collision-avoidance technology — a direct response to the January 2025 mid-air collision near Washington that killed 67 people. The bill now heads to the Senate, where bipartisan committee leaders have already flagged concerns it doesn't go far enough, so Roger should monitor the Senate version for any compliance requirements that could affect Breeze operations.

Skift reported on April 15th that Delta's chief commercial officer told reporters the airline is not interested in any merger, saying Delta doesn't need it and is successful on its own, even as speculation about a potential United-American combination continues to intensify. With Delta holding firm in a growth posture and United potentially consumed by merger conversations, Breeze may have a window to press its advantages in secondary markets that both legacy carriers have been slower to develop.

That's your briefing. Roger is standing by when you're ready to dive deeper or take action on any of these stories.
